6 min read
Just now

--

Before you build any model or draw any conclusion, you need to actually understand what you are looking at. This is how professionals do it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Exploratory Data Analysis, almost always shortened to EDA, is the process of investigating a dataset to understand its structure, find patterns, spot anomalies, and form early hypotheses before doing any serious modeling or drawing firm conclusions. If data cleaning is about making your data trustworthy, EDA is about actually getting to know it.
Skipping this step is one of the most common mistakes beginners make. They clean their data and jump straight to building a chart or a model, without ever really understanding what story the data is trying to tell. This article walks through a proper EDA process using the same kind of health data you have been working with throughout this series.
Why EDA Comes Before Everything Else
Imagine being handed a spreadsheet of ten thousand patient records and being asked to “find something interesting.” Without a structured approach, most people would not know where to start. EDA gives you that structure. It is a repeatable process for getting oriented in any new dataset, regardless of what it contains.
Good EDA answers questions like: How many records do I have? What does a typical record look like? Are there patterns across categories? Are there any surprising outliers? Do any two variables seem to move together? Answering these questions before building anything more sophisticated prevents you from building on a flawed or misunderstood foundation.
Setting Up a Realistic Dataset
Let us create a slightly larger, cleaner dataset to explore properly.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(42)
regions = [“Greater Accra”, “Ashanti”, “Northern”, “Western”, “Eastern”]
diseases = [“Malaria”, “Cholera”, “Typhoid”, “Tuberculosis”]
n = 500
data = {
“region”: np.random.choice(regions, n),
“disease”: np.random.choice(diseases, n, p=[0.5, 0.15, 0.2, 0.15]),
“age”: np.random.randint(1, 90, n),
“treatment_cost”: np.round(np.random.normal(120, 40, n), 2),
“recovery_days”: np.random.randint(2, 21, n)
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df[“treatment_cost”] = df[“treatment_cost”].clip(lower=10)
df.to_csv(“health_records.csv”, index=False)
print(“Sample dataset with 500 records created.”)
np.random.seed(42) makes your random data reproducible, meaning anyone running this exact code gets exactly the same “random” values. This matters enormously in real research, where reproducibility is essential. np.random.choice() with the p parameter lets us control the probability of each disease appearing, so Malaria naturally shows up most often, matching real world patterns. .clip(lower=10) ensures no treatment cost accidentally comes out negative.
Step 1: Get the Shape and Structure
Start every EDA the same way, understanding the basic dimensions and structure of what you are working with.
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv(“health_records.csv”)
print(f”Dataset shape: {df.shape}”)
print(f”Columns: {list(df.columns)}”)
print(df.dtypes)
df.shape returns a tuple of (rows, columns), immediately telling you the scale of what you are working with. Knowing you have 500 rows and 5 columns fundamentally shapes what kind of analysis makes sense. df.dtypes confirms every column has the data type you expect, catching any lingering formatting issues before you go further.
Step 2: Look at Central Tendency and Spread
For every numeric column, understand not just the average but how spread out the values are.
print(df[[“age”, “treatment_cost”, “recovery_days”]].describe())
.describe() gives you count, mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum, and the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles for every numeric column. The standard deviation tells you how much variation exists. A small standard deviation means most values cluster tightly around the mean. A large one means values are spread widely, which might be worth investigating further.
Pay particular attention to the minimum and maximum values here. This is often where you catch lingering data problems, an age of negative one, or a treatment cost of zero, that slipped past your cleaning process.
Step 3: Understand Categorical Distributions
For columns containing categories rather than numbers, value_counts() is your primary tool.
print(df[“region”].value_counts())
print(df[“disease”].value_counts())
This shows you exactly how many records fall into each category. Are the regions roughly evenly represented, or is one region dominating your dataset? This matters enormously for interpretation. If ninety percent of your records come from Greater Accra, any conclusion you draw is really a conclusion about Greater Accra, not about the whole country, regardless of how the data was originally framed.
You can also see this as percentages, which often communicates more clearly:
print(df[“disease”].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100)
Step 4: Group and Compare
This is where EDA starts producing real insight. Grouping lets you compare a metric across different categories.
print(df.groupby(“region”)[“treatment_cost”].mean())
.groupby(“region”) splits your DataFrame into separate groups, one for each unique region. [“treatment_cost”].mean() then calculates the average treatment cost within each of those groups separately. This single line answers a genuinely useful question: does treatment cost differ meaningfully by region?
You can group by multiple columns and calculate multiple statistics at once:
summary = df.groupby([“region”, “disease”])[“treatment_cost”].agg([“mean”, “count”])
print(summary)
This produces a detailed breakdown of average cost and number of cases for every combination of region and disease, immediately surfacing patterns that would be nearly impossible to spot by scrolling through raw rows manually.
Step 5: Look for Relationships Between Variables
Does age relate to recovery time? Does treatment cost relate to recovery time? Correlation gives you a quick first look, though as we will cover carefully in Article 11, correlation requires careful interpretation.
correlation = df[[“age”, “treatment_cost”, “recovery_days”]].corr()
print(correlation)
.corr() calculates the correlation coefficient between every pair of numeric columns, a number between negative one and positive one. Values close to positive one mean the two variables tend to increase together. Values close to negative one mean as one increases, the other tends to decrease. Values close to zero suggest little to no linear relationship.
Step 6: Identify Outliers
Outliers are values that sit unusually far from the rest of the data. They deserve investigation rather than automatic removal, since sometimes an outlier is the most important data point in your entire dataset, representing a genuine, important anomaly.
Q1 = df[“treatment_cost”].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df[“treatment_cost”].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 — Q1
lower_bound = Q1 — 1.5 * IQR
upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
outliers = df[(df[“treatment_cost”] < lower_bound) | (df[“treatment_cost”] > upper_bound)]
print(f”Number of outliers found: {len(outliers)}”)
print(outliers)
This uses the Interquartile Range, or IQR, method, one of the most standard statistical approaches for outlier detection. Q1 and Q3 mark the 25th and 75th percentiles. Anything falling more than 1.5 times the IQR below Q1 or above Q3 is flagged as an outlier. This is a widely used, defensible statistical convention, not an arbitrary guess.
Step 7: Ask Specific Research Questions
Generic exploration eventually needs to turn into specific questions relevant to your actual research interests. For a Health Informatics researcher, questions like these turn EDA into something directly useful:
Which region has the highest average treatment cost for Malaria specifically?
malaria_by_region = df[df[“disease”] == “Malaria”].groupby(“region”)[“treatment_cost”].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
print(malaria_by_region)
Does age group affect recovery time?
df[“age_group”] = pd.cut(df[“age”], bins=[0, 18, 35, 60, 100], labels=[“Child”, “Young Adult”, “Adult”, “Senior”])
recovery_by_age = df.groupby(“age_group”)[“recovery_days”].mean()
print(recovery_by_age)
pd.cut() is a useful function that converts a continuous numeric column, age, into discrete categories, or “bins”, making group comparisons far more interpretable than trying to compare every individual age value one by one.
What You Just Learned
Structural inspection. You learned to check the shape, columns, and data types of a dataset as your very first step.
Descriptive statistics. You learned to use describe() to understand central tendency and spread for numeric columns.
Categorical distributions. You learned to use value_counts() to understand how balanced or imbalanced your categorical data is.
Grouping and aggregation. You learned to use groupby() to compare metrics across categories, one of the single most useful techniques in all of data analysis.
Correlation. You got a first look at measuring relationships between numeric variables.
Outlier detection. You learned the IQR method, a standard, defensible statistical technique for flagging unusual values.
Binning continuous data. You learned to use pd.cut() to convert continuous numeric data into meaningful categories for easier comparison.
EDA Is a Mindset, Not Just a Checklist
The specific code in this article matters less than the mindset behind it. Good EDA is fundamentally about curiosity applied systematically. Every number in a dataset represents something real, a patient, a hospital visit, a cost. Approaching a dataset with genuine questions, rather than rushing toward a predetermined conclusion, is what separates trustworthy analysis from analysis that simply confirms whatever the analyst already believed going in.
This discipline will matter enormously in your future research. Before predicting outbreak risk, before building any model, before writing a single conclusion in a paper, this is the step that grounds everything that follows in an honest, careful understanding of what the data actually shows.
About the Author
Emmanuel Owusu Adu is a Computer Science graduate and researcher with interests spanning Health Informatics, Information Science, and Information Systems. He is passionate about leveraging technology and data to transform healthcare delivery and digital systems across Africa.
